oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Ask the Vet?
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: DVM, Stephen Fronefield,
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 1 (January/February 2024).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Avian Influenza: The Nuts and Boltsby Stephen Fronefield, DVM ABC Animal and Bird Clinic, Sugarland, Texas https://myabcvet.com/ What is Avian Influenza (AI)? Avian influenza is a flu virus of birds. Other names associated with the virus include Bird Flu, Influenza type A, Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza A(HPAI), it is further subclassified by the subtype, H5N1, and to get further into the weeds, this virus ’ clad is 2.3.4.4b. It can be written as 2.3.4.4b HPAI A(H5N1) to most completely identify the virus associated with the current outbreak.This virus was introduced into North America in late 2021. Unlike previous AI outbreaks, which tended to flourish in the winter months then wane in spring, this virus has continued to affect birds throughout the year.Which birds are affected? Avian influenza A viruses have been isolated from more than 100 different species of birds around the world. Wild birds that can be infected include waterbirds like ducks, swans, geese, gulls, and terns, and shorebirds, like storks, sandpipers, and plovers. Wild aquatic birds, especially dabbling ducks, are considered reservoirs for avian influenza viruses. Significant concern exists because raptors, California condors, and the African penguin, amongst other species, are susceptible to the virus.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 1 (2024): Watchbird January/February 2024; 18-19
